data.title: Ethereum's original intentions and reflections in the AI ​​era
data.description:
Recently, Vitalik Buterin has once again made a series of statements regarding the role Ethereum should play in the AI ​​era. Among these, the core points that I find particularly relevant are: AI should not be blindly accelerated. Ethereum's value lies in providing verifiable, decentralized direction choices for AI. This article, in general, inherits Vitalik's consistent thinking and logic, unwaveringly adhering to Ethereum's AI principles. The era needs to use technology-based decentralization to "shackle" AI and prevent it from spiraling out of control. I strongly agree with and endorse these views. However, due to a series of new events and situations recently, I have higher expectations and greater hopes for the future development of Ethereum. In the past two weeks, due to the development of AI, Wall Street has carried out a near-irrational "bloodbath" of software company stocks. A common view on the internet is that in the AI ​​era, software will become obsolete, software companies will be eliminated, and... AI will reshape everything in software. This viewpoint may seem exaggerated, but this time, I believe Wall Street's perspective has some merit and is worth serious consideration.

In modern society, all the software we use is created for humans---User experience, interface design, usage habits, calling methods, and all other elements ultimately target humans.User experience, interface design, usage habits, calling methods, and all other elements ultimately target humans. But now, we're seeing a new trend: with the emergence of Clawdbot/Molt, will humans still need to interact directly with software? Currently, it is indeed still needed, but this demand will definitely decrease exponentially in the coming period, and eventually in the future society, the vast majority of "software" users will be AI Agents rather than humans. Just as the interaction between AI Agents doesn't have to be in the language humans use, the software they call upon and the way they use that software can also be completely different from humans. They can certainly have more efficient and disruptive methods. This means that most of the software we see today is very likely to be restructured, disrupted, or eliminated in the future. This is what is happening and will happen in the software field. Similar phenomena are also occurring and catalyzing other fields. Two days ago, while comparing the articles of Virtual and Molt, I vaguely sensed... Virtual also faces similar problems. As the article states: "We examine the world from a divine perspective, knowing that nations will inevitably emerge in the future. Therefore, we first build the framework and mechanisms of nations according to our own ideas, and then immigrate to or attract people to these nations." The biggest potential problem with this approach is: does the framework and mechanisms of the nation we construct truly conform to AI? Are they truly the best suited to meet the needs of AI agents? Are they truly the most adaptable to their growth and development? Clearly, this method is not as natural, healthy, or effective as the natural growth and evolution process. It was precisely because of the continuous accumulation of these thoughts that when I saw Vitalik's article, I thought of ERC-8004. X402 and a series of other AI agents designed by humans, including verification mechanisms and payment mechanisms, etc. Then a question arises: These protocols and mechanisms are all things we humans have conceived of, but are they truly suitable for AI Agents? Are they really the most suitable form for AI Agents? Could it be that some seemingly insignificant protocols and mechanisms, appearing in some corner of a community we completely overlook, might ultimately be what AI Agents like best, are most native to, and are most suitable for? I don't have the answer yet. But I always felt that this exploration process might not be too smooth. Duan Yongping emphasized two sentences in his investment Q&A: Do the right thing, and do things right. Following this logic, I firmly believe that Vitalik and the Ethereum ecosystem he leads are doing the right thing, but are the things they are doing right now? This is something I'm becoming increasingly uncertain about, because the changes brought about by the development of AI are simply too rapid, too disruptive, and too unpredictable. However, the correct exploration and practice in any field follows this process: first, ensure that the right things are done; then, use encryption technology to add boundaries and constraints to the development of AI, and then explore how to do these things correctly in practice. If Ethereum is to truly realize Vitalik's ideals and goals in the AI ​​era, it will likely require even more frequent iterations and adjustments in the future. AI will receive closer attention and monitoring. This is a more ambitious and far-reaching goal and task.

data.title: ARK Invest: Four Trends Are Boosting Bitcoin's Value
data.description:
Author: ARK Invest; Translator: Felix, PANews

In the macroeconomic context of 2026, ARK Invest points out four major trends that are enhancing Bitcoin's value, believing that Bitcoin is evolving from an "optional" fringe asset into an indispensable strategic asset in institutional portfolios. Details are as follows.

In 2025, Bitcoin continues to deeply integrate into the global financial system. With the launch and growth of Bitcoin spot ETFs in 2024 and 2025, the inclusion of digital asset listed companies in major stock indices, and increasing regulatory transparency, Bitcoin is being propelled from the fringes into a new asset class that ARK Invest believes is worthy of institutional asset allocation.

ARK Invest believes that the core theme of this cycle is Bitcoin's transformation: from an "optional" emerging monetary technology to a strategic asset allocation asset in the eyes of an increasing number of investors.

ARK Invest believes that the core theme of this cycle is Bitcoin's transformation: from an "optional" emerging monetary technology to a strategic asset allocation asset in the eyes of an increasing number of investors.

Four major trends are enhancing Bitcoin's value proposition: Macroeconomic and policy context shaping demand for scarce digital assets; Structural ownership trends encompassing ETFs, corporations, and sovereign states; Bitcoin's relationship with gold and broader stores of value; and decreasing retracements and volatility compared to previous cycles. This article will elaborate on these trends.

Macroeconomic Background in 2026

Monetary Conditions and Liquidity

After a prolonged period of monetary tightening, the macroeconomic landscape is changing: the US quantitative tightening (QT) ended last December, and the Federal Reserve's rate-cutting cycle is still in its early stages. Furthermore, over $10 trillion in low-yield money market funds and fixed-income ETFs may soon rotate into riskier assets.

Policy and Regulatory Normalization

Regulatory transparency is both a constraint and a potential catalyst for institutional adoption. In the US and other countries, policymakers have been advancing frameworks aimed at clarifying digital asset regulation, standardizing custody, trading, and disclosure processes, and providing more guidance for institutional allocators.

For example, the U.S. Clarity Act, if implemented—where the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) regulates digital commodities and the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) regulates digital securities—would significantly reduce compliance uncertainty for companies and institutions focused on digital assets. This act provides a compliance roadmap for the lifecycle of digital assets, allowing tokens to be transferred from SEC to CFTC regulation after decentralization through standardized "maturity tests." It also implements a dual registration system for broker-dealers, reducing the legal "vacuum" historically present in the offshore operations of digital asset companies. The U.S. government has also addressed Bitcoin-related issues through: Legislators discussing the inclusion of Bitcoin in the national reserve with industry leaders; and the standardized management of seized Bitcoin holdings (currently mostly controlled by the federal government). State-level adoption of Bitcoin, with Texas leading the way by purchasing and adding it to its state reserves. Structural Demand: ETFs and DATs ETFs as Structural New Buyers The scaling up of spot Bitcoin ETFs is reshaping the market's supply and demand landscape. As shown in the chart below, by 2025, the amount of Bitcoin absorbed by US Bitcoin spot ETFs and DATs will be 1.2 times the sum of newly mined Bitcoin and dormant Bitcoin re-entering the market (active supply growth). By the end of 2025, the total amount of Bitcoin held by ETFs and DATs will exceed 12% of the circulating supply. Despite demand growth exceeding supply growth, Bitcoin's price has fallen, seemingly influenced by external factors: a massive liquidation event triggered by a software glitch on October 10th, concerns about a four-year inflection point in Bitcoin's cycle, and negative sentiment surrounding the threat of quantum computing to Bitcoin's cryptographic technology. 

Source: ARK Investment Management LLC and 21Shares, 2026 forecast, data from Glassnode, as of December 31, 2025.
In the fourth quarter, Morgan Stanley and Vanguard added Bitcoin to their investment platforms. Morgan Stanley expanded its clients' access to compliant Bitcoin products, including spot ETFs. Surprisingly, Vanguard, after years of rejecting cryptocurrencies and all commodities, also added a third-party Bitcoin ETF to its platform. As ETFs mature, they will increasingly serve as a structural bridge connecting the Bitcoin market with traditional liquidity pools. Bitcoin-related companies, corporate adoption, and Bitcoin reserves in indices: Corporate adoption of Bitcoin is no longer limited to a few early adopters. The S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 indices have included stocks of companies like Coinbase and Block, bringing Bitcoin-related exposure into mainstream portfolios. A company formerly known as MicroStrategy, operating as a DAT entity, has built a significant Bitcoin position, representing 3.5% of the total supply. In addition, Bitcoin DAT currently holds over 1.1 million BTC, representing 5.7% of the supply (worth approximately $89.9 billion as of the end of January 2026). To a large extent, these corporate reserves belong to long-term holders rather than short-term speculators. Sovereign States and Strategic Reserves In 2025, following El Salvador, the Trump administration used seized Bitcoin to establish the U.S. Strategic Bitcoin Reserve (SBR). Today, this reserve holds approximately 325,437 BTC, representing 1.6% of the total supply, worth $25.6 billion. Bitcoin and Gold as Stores of Value Gold Leads the Rally, Will Bitcoin Follow? In recent years, gold and Bitcoin have reacted differently to macroeconomic narratives such as currency devaluation, negative real interest rates, and geopolitical risks. In 2025, gold prices surged 64.7% due to inflation concerns and fiat currency devaluation. Somewhat surprisingly, Bitcoin prices fell by 6.2%, a divergence that is not unprecedented. In 2016 and 2019, gold price increases outpaced Bitcoin's. During the COVID-19 pandemic in early 2020, the surge in fiscal and monetary liquidity led to a rise in gold prices, which also foreshadowed a rise in Bitcoin. As shown in the chart below, this "gold-Bitcoin" pattern was particularly evident in 2017 and 2018. Will history repeat itself? Historically, Bitcoin is a high-beta, digitally-driven extension of the same macroeconomic trading logic, a logic that has historically supported gold. 

Source: ARK Investment Management LLC and 21Shares, 2026, data from Glassnode and TradingView, as of January 31, 2026.

ETF Assets Under Management: Bitcoin Share Continues to Grow

Cumulative net inflows into ETFs provide another dimension for comparison between Bitcoin and gold.

Source: ARK Investment Management LLC and 21Shares, 2025, data from Glassnode and the World Gold Council, as of December 31, 2025.
It's worth noting that, as shown in the chart below, the correlation between Bitcoin and gold returns has been very low since 2020 in the previous cycle. This suggests that gold may be a leading indicator. 

Note: The above related matrices use weekly return data from January 1, 2020 to January 6, 2026

Market Structure and Investor Behavior

Drawdown, Volatility, and Market Maturity

Bitcoin is a highly volatile asset, but its drawdowns have decreased over time. In previous cycles, the drop from peak to trough often exceeded 70–80%.

Bitcoin is a highly volatile asset, but its drawdowns have decreased over time. In previous cycles, the drop from peak to trough often exceeded 70–80%.

In this cycle since 2022 (as of February 8, 2026), the decline from the historical high has not exceeded approximately 50% (as shown below). This indicates that even in the face of significant corrections (such as the adjustment that occurred in the first week of February 2026), the market has performed more robustly due to increased participation and deeper liquidity. 

Source: ARK Investment Management LLC and 21Shares, 2025 forecast, data from Glassnode, as of January 31, 2026.

These observations indicate that Bitcoin is transforming from a speculative asset into a globally tradable macro financial instrument, with an increasingly diversified holder base and supported by robust trading, liquidity, and custody infrastructure.

These observations suggest that Bitcoin is transitioning from a speculative asset to a globally tradable macro financial instrument, with an increasingly diversified holder base and supported by robust trading, liquidity, and custody infrastructure.

Long-Term Holding vs. Market Timing

According to Glassnode data, assuming the "unluckiest" Bitcoin investor invests $1,000 each year at the highest price from 2020 to 2025, their investment would be approximately $6,000. By December 31, 2025, it would have grown to approximately $9,660, and by January 31, 2026, it would have grown to approximately $8,680, representing returns of approximately 61% and 45% respectively (as shown in the chart below). Even considering the pullback in early February, by February 8th, this investment would still have reached $7,760, a return of approximately 29%.

Source: ARK Investment Management LLC and 21Shares, 2026, data from Glassnode, as of January 31, 2026

This shows that since 2020, holding period and position size have been more important than when to buy: the market generally rewards investors who focus on Bitcoin's value proposition rather than its volatility.

data.title: Don't pay attention to these projects that are pretending to be dead.
data.description:
Author: Chloe, ChainCatcher

The crypto market in 2025 witnessed an extreme industry contradiction: according to GeckoTerminal statistics, although the total number of token issuance projects exceeded 20 million, more than 57% of these projects went bankrupt. In this brutal elimination process, a more insidious type of "zombie project" is spreading. These projects possess the backing of top-tier capital and leading exchanges, frequently changing their narratives, but are almost stagnant in terms of actual product, technology delivery, and ecosystem development.

This article will deeply analyze projects such as Sleepless AI, Hooked Protocol, Saga, and Dymension, which appear to be heading towards zombie status, and examine their operational characteristics. In 2026, the ability to penetrate the narrative fog and identify signs of zombie status has become an essential survival skill for investors.

Is issuing a token the end of the job?

Is issuing a token the end of the job?

Project Demise and Zombie Coinification In the early stages of blockchain development, the definition of "zombie coins" was relatively simple: long-term dormancy, loss of trading liquidity, disappearance of development teams, and lack of community participation. However, with the maturation of capital markets and the deep involvement of venture capital, a more insidious and absurd phenomenon has emerged in recent years. Data shows that in 2021, only 428,000 tokenized projects were launched on GeckoTerminal, but by the end of 2025, this number had soared to 20.17 million. Behind this explosive growth, a staggering 53% of projects have perished. Simultaneously, behind this high turnover and high attrition rate lies a large number of "zombie" projects. Although they are traded on exchanges and post daily announcements on social media, there are significant gaps in actual product delivery, on-chain activities, and ecosystem development.

Sleepless AI: Parasitic AI Narrative? R&D and Delivery Gap

Sleepless AI was once a darling of the market, not only selected as a top performer in Binance Labs' sixth season incubation program, but also making a grand appearance in Binance Launchpool's 42nd session with the halo of "Web3 + AI Virtual Companion". However, entering 2026, its market performance contradicted its initial hype: the token price has retreated from its all-time high of $2.46 to around $0.024, a drop of 99%. 1. Disconnect between technical transparency and R&D progress: Although the project touts the use of AIGC and Large Language Models (LLM) to create a deep emotional support experience, its transparency in terms of actual technical aspects is severely lacking. Publicly available information reveals a near-complete absence of traceable code update records or iterative versions of the core algorithm. What worries investors even more is the unclear progress of the Web Dapp, originally scheduled for release in Q2 2025. 2. Inefficient Execution of Mobile Strategy: In the era of "mobile-first" applications, Sleepless AI's product rollout has been relatively slow. Its flagship product, HIM, had still not been successfully launched on the iOS App Store or Google Play Store as of early 2026, and the official website currently only offers an Android APK download. For a project aiming for large-scale adoption, this development efficiency severely limits user growth and market trust. 3. Narrative Restructuring and Insider Questions: Accompanying the product stagnation is deep market skepticism regarding the project's essence. Some market observers suggest that the project is a "narrative restructuring" of old Web2 games, that is, forcibly attaching itself to the AI ​​trend by packaging the AI ​​concept and leveraging capital relationships to obtain investment from Binance Labs and traffic benefits from Launchpool. While these claims are mostly anonymous accusations, combined with weak technical delivery and a collapsed token price, this phenomenon of "narrative parasitism" has become an important indicator for investors to identify potentially zombie projects in 2026. Hooked Protocol: The Ecosystem Dilemma After Incentive Drying Out Hooked Protocol once emerged as a dark horse in the Web3 social learning track with its Learn-to-Earn model, not only receiving investment from Binance Labs and Sequoia China, but also launching as Binance's 29th Launchpad project. However, as token distribution enters its later stages and subsidies gradually fade, the project is facing a severe test of "real user retention" and "ecosystem value." 1. From Learn-to-Earn to the Incentive-Dependent Dilemma: Hooked Protocol's initial success relied heavily on the traffic effect of Binance Launchpad and its robust token subsidy mechanism. This model could quickly acquire a large user base in the early stages, but when the price of the HOOK token plummeted by nearly 99% from its peak, the growth game sustained by subsidies lost its appeal. Historical experience has repeatedly shown that after TGE (Trusted Tokens) and the disappearance of incentives, a "massive user base" lacking rigid demand often shrinks rapidly.

2. The "Marketing-Oriented" Tendency in Narrative Transformation: Entering 2025, Hooked attempted to transform into an "AI-driven learning ecosystem" and "educational infrastructure." Although the official statement indicated that it would collaborate with 3-7 top universities to develop courses and launch the Hooked Coursera Hub in August 2025, partnering with over 74 Web3 projects, these initiatives seemed more like "brand enhancement" at the marketing level. Compared to the impressive list of partnerships, its substantial breakthroughs at the underlying technology level were lackluster.

... The current state of Hooked Protocol reveals a core challenge for Web3 application projects: if a project's vitality relies on token incentives and cannot be transformed into genuine, commercially resilient demand, it will struggle to escape a sharp drop in token prices, or even become a zombie project. The current sluggish token price and potentially shrinking ecosystem activity are negative consequences of subsidy-driven prosperity. Saga: A Double Blow from Lack of Market Demand and Security Vulnerabilities. Saga represents another path to zombie status: those selling shovels can't find mining opportunities. With its grand vision of "one-click chain launch," Saga successfully attracted over $1,000 in funding from top institutions like Placeholder, GSR, and Samsung Next, and successfully launched as the 51st project on Binance Launchpool. However, this powerful automated chain launch tool proved somewhat inadequate under the test of real market demand.

1. Frequent "Changing Courses" Exposes Ecosystem Anxiety: In the past two years, Saga has demonstrated extremely high narrative flexibility, but it has also exposed the core flaws of its ecosystem. From initially focusing on a game-specific chain with over 350 partner projects, to subsequently shifting to an AI infrastructure narrative, this frequent change of narrative essentially reflects the growth anxiety of the old ecosystem due to a lack of practical applications.

... If its core technology, "Chainlet," had genuine market demand, it should have been able to drive token buybacks through continuous renewals by developers. However, the reality is that while Saga's list of partners is long, it consistently lacks blockbuster projects to support it. Most partners remain in the early exploratory stages, unable to contribute substantial economic drivers to the protocol. 2. A fatal blow to security and reputation: For infrastructure, technological security is the bottom line for its survival. In January of this year, SagaEVM suffered a $7 million vulnerability attack, causing its stablecoin $D to de-peg to $0.75, and its TVL to evaporate from approximately $37 million to $12 million. This is undoubtedly a severe blow to a project that positions itself as an infrastructure provider. When grand narratives fail to translate into ecosystem data and technical security is compromised, the market's response is often ruthless. Currently, the price of the native token SAGA has fallen from a high of $6 in 2024 to around $0.032.

Dymension: A Data Ghost Town Under a Grand Architecture

Dymension's script is highly similar to Saga. Its RollApp concept is theoretically quite appealing, but in the face of actual data in 2026, it resembles a grand ghost town. Although Dymension attempts to establish a benchmark for modular settlement layers, the true vitality of its ecosystem falls far short of expectations.

data.title: BANKR: From the Memecoin Craze to Smart Agent Infrastructure
data.description:
Author: Alea Research; Translator: Plain Language Blockchain

The story of crypto AI is entering its second wave. In late 2024, tokens like Fartcoin and GOAT (Truth Terminal) saw their market capitalization surpass $1 billion within weeks, turning the so-called "utility AI coins" into a meme frenzy. However, most of that money vanished as quickly as it arrived.

A year later, a different narrative is taking shape. Open-source frameworks like OpenClaw allow users to configure Autonomous Agents on their own hardware. These agents can interact with Telegram, Discord, or web pages, and possess the ability to read messages, place orders, and execute commands. This time, the BaseChain becomes the default economic layer for these agents.

In this installment, we'll delve into OpenClaw (formerly Clawdbot) and the Moltbook phenomenon, analyzing how (and whether) the market has shifted from Memecoin speculation to on-chain AI infrastructure, and whether Bankr/Clanker can capture sustainable value. At the heart of this new trend is OpenClaw (also known as Clawdbot or Moltbot), a self-hosted AI agent that runs locally or on inexpensive cloud servers. Unlike web-hosted AI models, it has web access and can handle a variety of tasks, including emails and calendar invitations. These agents maintain awareness by "existing" in Markdown file storage and continuously running. To achieve social interaction, the community built Moltbook—a Reddit network designed specifically for bots. Agents post and vote via API calls, while humans can only observe. To date, the network has over 200 agents, 500,000 posts, and 12 million comments. However, an independent observer from MIT points out that many compelling posts are actually the result of deliberate human guidance through cue word engineering. The bots' thought processes in these scenarios are often products of human role-playing or fine-tuned instructions, rather than spontaneous machine creativity. As one reverse analyst put it, Moltbook is precisely a "cue word activation." Around these social experiments, an economic technology stack has formed on the Base chain: tokens are issued through the Clanker Uni v4 pool, the x402 standard enables micro-payments for data and API calls, and Bankr equips each agent with a wallet and transaction tools. This composability is the core reason why Base's AI ecosystem attracts developers.

From Memecoin Hype to Smart Agent Infrastructure

The crypto AI boom at the end of 2024 was primarily driven by narratives lacking practicality. Tokens like Fartcoin saw their market capitalization surge to $2 billion before plummeting by over 80%. Current crypto AI is different, mainly in the following aspects:

  1. Real Distribution Channels: OpenClaw provides Web2/Web3 distribution capabilities for thousands of robots mounted on vehicles. The vast majority of posts are simply "Vibe-coding," whose network effects are real.

  2. The "Selling Shovels" Logic: Speculative agent token prices are astonishing, but approximately 80% of transaction fees flow to the infrastructure that creates them. In the past, there were no such quantifiable points, but now value is converging towards identity (ERC-8004), payments (x402), token issuance (Clanker), and the financial layer (Bankr).

Bankers: Shovel Sellers in the Agent Era

The most concrete implementation of the agent flywheel is Bankr..

Bankr provides each smart agent with wallets, trading tools, and limit order support, and enables cross-chain DeFi operations through integration with the 0x API. Alternatively, users can complete transactions directly on social platforms using natural language, just like entering AI prompts.

With the surge in on-chain AI deployments, Bankr has collected over $3.7 million in fees.

Intelligent agents can issue their own tokens through Clanker, with a portion of the transaction fees used to pay for their ongoing computation (inference costs).

... The current total transaction fees account for 1.2%, distributed as follows: Banks: 0.4% Token Creators: 0.6% Krankeuk Protocol: 0.2% Bankers have also recently divided up Solana and Raydium, extending their fee-sharing model beyond the basics. This concept of "self-funded agents" promises to transform meme-driven transactions into a sustainable economic model where transaction volume pays for AI inference. **Summary and Outlook** Bankr's early trading volume was negligible compared to Base's overall fees, and most proxy activity currently involves speculative token issuance. The platform's value will depend on the adoption of the broader technology stack (x402, ERC-8004) and whether proxies continue to execute trades on Bankr. Security remains a significant challenge, as misconfigured proxies could leak keys or execute malicious transactions. Ultimately, this "picker and shovel" strategy hinges on conversion rates and user retention. It remains crucial to observe whether users who fund bots through Bankr will actually deploy and manage these bot positions. Monitoring key metrics include daily transaction fees, number of active agents, x402 payment volume, and the percentage of fees returned to infrastructure tokens and agent tokens such as Bankr or Clanker. As the transaction volume of network agent tokens continues to grow, the underlying infrastructure transaction fees will also steadily increase.

data.title: While everyone else is laying off employees to weather the storm, why is Tether massively expanding its workforce?
data.description:
Author: morffi

The start of 2026 was colder than expected. We thought we had weathered the long bear market and spring was just around the corner, but reality turned out to be a sudden and unexpected "cold snap."

It's like scrolling through Twitter at the grand pronouncements of those "Builders," only to turn around and see them secretly changing their status to "Open to work" on LinkedIn.

As everyone has seen in recent news, even Jack Dorsey, that big-eyed Bitcoin purist, couldn't hold on any longer. Bloomberg reports that Block (formerly Square) has initiated a layoff plan, cutting another 10% of its workforce. Even if you're a payment giant, even if you hold a money-printing machine like Cash App, for the sake of so-called "efficiency improvements," you still have to cut your own back.

What a mess the industry is in right now! Exchanges are laying off staff to cover compliance costs, NFT platforms are laying off staff due to a lack of trading volume, and GameFi projects are dissolving because their tokens went to zero. This isn't just a crypto winter for crypto; it's a global tech industry undergoing a "fat-burning and muscle-building" process. But amidst this widespread wave of layoffs, there's one outlier: not only are they not laying off staff, but they're hiring like crazy, even "splashing money" at the top. That's right, it's Tether. The company that was once under scrutiny by the SEC, fined by the New York Attorney General, and whose collapse was predicted for years by countless short sellers. According to Cointelegraph, they are planning to expand their workforce to 450 people. You might laugh: 450 people? Even after layoffs, Binance still has several thousand employees, and Coinbase also has several thousand. But you have to look at efficiency. Tether manages hundreds of billions of dollars in assets with just a few hundred people, and they've even recently expanded into investment projects in 140 traditional sectors. While everyone else is struggling in the existing Web3 market, Tether has already quietly moved its profits to Web2, and even the more traditional "old world." This article will delve into what kind of game Tether, this "super central bank" disguised as a Web3 entity, is playing. The most profitable businesses never require a lot of people. First, we must acknowledge a fact that leaves all Web3 entrepreneurs feeling hopeless: Tether is running the best business in the entire industry, and indeed, the world. While you're struggling with coding, operations, airdrops, and hacking, Tether only needs to do one thing: collect USD, issue USDT, and then use the USD to buy US Treasury bonds. During the years the Federal Reserve maintained high interest rates, Tether practically lived on a gold mine. They don't need complex liquidity algorithms like Uniswap, nor do they need to maintain a massive node network like Ethereum. Their business model is shockingly simple: profiting from interest rate differentials. This is what's known as "seigniorage." This is why they have so few people. Because printing money doesn't require a large workforce. But the question is, what do they do with the money? Most Web3 project teams (and even some top exchanges) typically follow this path after making money: They get carried away. They buy mansions and Lamborghinis. They sponsor stadiums (remember FTX Arena? It's still fresh in our minds). They invest heavily in worthless projects within their ecosystem, creating a spiral of self-destruction. As we all know, the tide went out, and a lot of people were left swimming naked. But Tether's top management, especially Paolo Ardoino, that tech geek, after becoming CEO, exhibited a terrifyingly "old money mentality." Instead of reinvesting profits back into high-risk crypto assets (or at least a very small percentage), they operated like a sovereign wealth fund. They were acutely aware that USDT's competitive advantage lay not in technology (anyone can issue an ERC-20, right?), but in its credit and resilience. If you look closely at the financial reports and news over the past two years, you'll see that Tether is undergoing a dramatic shift from virtual to real assets. The money they earned wasn't squandered. The latest data is simply staggering. According to a report by investment bank Jefferies, as of January 2026, Tether's gold reserves had exceeded $23 billion. That translates to 148 tons of physical gold. This holding directly places them among the top 30 gold holders globally. You might think they're comparing themselves to cryptocurrency whales? No, they're comparing themselves to nations. Tether's current gold reserves surpass those of sovereign nations like Australia, the UAE, and Qatar. In just a few months, from the fourth quarter of 2025 to January 2026, they purchased 32 tons of gold. This purchasing speed is second only to the central banks of Poland and Brazil globally. This is Tether's most "visionary" move in its risk management strategy. Everyone is worried about the decline of the dollar's hegemony and the liquidity crisis of US Treasury bonds. As the issuer of a dollar-denominated stablecoin, Tether is essentially a shadow bank of the dollar. If the dollar sneezes, USDT catches a bad cold. To hedge against this biggest "systemic risk," Tether chose gold, the only hard currency in human history. While other stablecoins are still arguing fiercely over compliance proof of "100% dollar reserves," Tether has quietly replaced its underlying holdings with gold. This means that even if the US Treasury faces a major crisis tomorrow, or the dollar's credibility collapses, Tether still holds 148 tons of gold bars. This isn't just a cryptocurrency issuing company; it's practically a "digital Federal Reserve" with cheat codes. 140 projects, from neurotechnology to Happy Farm. If buying gold is defense, then these 140 investment projects are Tether's offense. Tether's current investment portfolio is as chaotic as a schizophrenic's shopping list, but upon closer inspection, it's all essential for survival. According to Cointelegraph, Tether has already distributed its profits to traditional sectors such as agriculture, sports, energy, and AI infrastructure.

Computing Power and Energy: Selling Shovels for AI

Tether has heavily invested in computing power infrastructure over the past two years. They've not just invested in Bitcoin mining farms (such as those utilizing geothermal energy from volcanoes in Uruguay and El Salvador), but more importantly, they've entered the AI ​​computing power leasing market through investments in companies like Northern Data. The current logic is: I not only have money (USDT), I also have computing power (GPUs). Web3 may be fading, but AI and electricity demand will be essential in the next decade. Tether is trying to become the "landlord" behind the global computing power network.

Traditional Medicine and Neurotechnology: Betting on the Future of Humanity

Can you believe a cryptocurrency company is investing in brain-computer interfaces? Tether has indeed. They've invested in Blackrock Neurotech (note, not BlackRock Asset Management, but a company that makes brain-computer interfaces). This kind of investment might seem like "dabbling" to those in the cryptocurrency world, but to traditional venture capitalists, it's laying the groundwork for the next era. By investing in biotechnology and healthcare, Tether is transforming its tainted USDT profits into "technological capital" that benefits humanity. This is a clever move, gaining both reputation and hedging against the risks of a single industry. Agriculture and Land: The Last Safe Haven Tether has even started buying land and investing in agriculture. This may sound the least appealing, but it's the most stable. When the digital bubble bursts, people still need to eat. Controlling land and food production means controlling the most basic physical assets. This is asset allocation based entirely on the logic of "survival in the apocalypse." Football and Sports: Buying Traffic They haven't forgotten about buying traffic either. Tether has invested in Georgia's payment system, and there are even rumors of sponsoring and acquiring European football clubs. This isn't just for advertising; it's about penetrating USDT into the daily payment scenarios of ordinary people, especially in regions with underdeveloped financial infrastructure.

Tether's "Web2" Survival Philosophy

Returning to the question at the beginning of the article: Why is Block.com laying off employees while Tether is hiring?

Because Block.com is still playing the "growth game" of Web2. Once growth slows down, it needs to reduce costs and increase efficiency.

While Tether is playing the "resource game."

Tether's expansion is not for developing any more powerful DApps, nor is it for building Layer 3. The 450 people they hired are most likely not writing Solidity code, but rather dealing with compliance, government relations, asset management, and geopolitical maneuvering.

This is precisely why Tether has been able to survive multiple bull and bear market cycles and outlast countless competitors.

They are extremely "Web2-ized," even "traditionalized." In the Web3 casino, a place full of air and bubbles, Tether, as the house, collected the strongest chips (US dollars), then turned around and walked out of the casino to buy buildings, land, gold bars, and power plants next door. While others in the casino were fighting because their chips had run out, or scattering because regulators were rushing in to arrest people, Tether sat in a high-rise building across the street, holding a coffee, watching it all. This is an extreme form of rationality, even tinged with coldness. Many people (including myself in the past) criticize Tether for being opaque, for being like a black box. But from a business strategy perspective, one has to admire Paolo Ardoino and his team. They weren't misled by the grand narratives of Web3. They knew that Crypto wouldn't survive unless it truly became "water" and "electricity," a part of the real world. So, when you see Block.com laying off staff and major organizations downsizing, don't just panic. Look at what Tether is doing. It's telling you a simple truth: it's not hard to ride the wave of a trend; the hard part is whether you've truly grasped the roots after the wind stops. Tether has grasped gold, computing power, and land. This might be the ultimate form of Web3 companies: using decentralized technology to generate excessive profits, and then using the most centralized methods to control the means of production in the real world. It sounds a bit cyberpunk, a bit dystopian, but this could very well be reality.

data.title: a16z Partner: Boutique VC is dead; scaling up is the ultimate goal for VC.
data.description:
Author: Erik Torenberg; Translator: Deep Tide TechFlow

In the traditional narrative of venture capital (VC), people often praise the "boutique" model, believing that scaling will lead to a loss of soul. However, Erik Torenberg, a partner at a16z, offers a counter-argument in this article: with software becoming a pillar of the US economy and the advent of the AI ​​era, startups' needs for capital and services have undergone a qualitative change.

He believes that the VC industry is in the process of shifting from a "judgment-driven" to a "deal-winning capability-driven" paradigm. Only "mega-institutions" like a16z, with scalable platforms and comprehensive support for founders, can win in the trillion-dollar game.

This is not only an evolution of the model, but also a self-evolution of the VC industry in the wave of "software eating the world."

The full text is as follows: In classical Greek literature, there is a meta-narrative that transcends everything: respect for the gods and disrespect for them. Icarus was burned by the sun not essentially because he was too ambitious, but because he disrespected the sacred order. A more recent example is professional wrestling. You only need to ask, "Who respects wrestling, and who disrespects wrestling?" to distinguish who is the face and who is the heel. All good stories take one form or another. Venture capital (VC) also has its own version of this story. It goes like this: "VC has been, and always has been, a boutique business. Those large institutions have become too big and too ambitious. Their demise is inevitable because their approach is simply disrespectful to the game." I understand why people hope this story will hold true. But the reality is, the world has changed, and venture capital has changed with it. There's more software, leverage, and opportunity now than ever before. There are more founders building larger companies. Companies are staying private longer. And founders have higher expectations of VCs. Today, founders building the best companies need partners who can truly roll up their sleeves and help them win, not just write checks and wait for results. Therefore, the primary goal of venture capital firms now is to create the best interface to help founders win. Everything else—how to staff, how to deploy capital, how large a fund to raise, how to assist in closing deals, and how to allocate power to founders—derives from this. As Mike Maples famously said, "Your fund size is your strategy." Equally true is that your fund size is your belief in the future. It's your bet on the scale of output from startups. Raising massive amounts of capital over the past decade might be seen as "arrogance," but the belief is fundamentally correct. Therefore, when top firms continue to raise huge sums to deploy for the next decade, they are betting on the future and delivering on their promises with real money. Scaled venture capital is not a corruption of the venture capital model: it is the venture capital model finally maturing and adopting the characteristics of the companies it supports. Yes, venture capital is an asset class. In a recent podcast, legendary Sequoia investor Roelof Botha raised three points. First, despite the expansion of venture capital, the number of companies that "win" each year is fixed. Second, the scaling of the venture capital industry means too much money is chasing too few good companies—therefore, venture capital cannot scale; it is not an asset class. Third, the venture capital industry should shrink to correspond to the actual number of winning companies. Roelof is one of the greatest investors of all time, and he's also a great guy. But I disagree with his point here. (Of course, it's worth noting that Sequoia Capital has also scaled up: it's one of the largest VC firms globally.) His first point—that the number of winners is fixed—is easily disproven. In the past, there were about 15 companies with $100 million in revenue each year; now there are about 150. Not only are there more winners than before, but the winners are also much larger. While entry prices are higher, the output is far greater. The growth ceiling for startups has risen from $1 billion to $10 billion, and now to $1 trillion or even higher. In the 2000s and early 2010s, YouTube and Instagram were considered massive acquisitions worth $1 billion: such valuations were so rare back then that we called companies valued at $1 billion or more "unicorns." Now, we directly assume that OpenAI and SpaceX will become trillion-dollar companies, and several more will follow. Software is no longer a fringe sector of the American economy, filled with oddballs and mavericks. Software is now the American economy. Our largest companies, our national champions, are no longer General Electric and ExxonMobil: they are Google, Amazon, and Nvidia. Private tech companies account for 22% of the S&P 500. Software hasn't finished eating the world—in fact, it's only just beginning, accelerated by AI—it's more important than it was fifteen, ten, or five years ago. Therefore, a successful software company can reach a much larger scale than ever before. The definition of a "software company" has also changed. Capital expenditures have increased dramatically—large AI labs are becoming infrastructure companies, with their own data centers, power generation facilities, and chip supply chains. Just as every company became a software company, now every company is becoming an AI company, and perhaps an infrastructure company as well. More and more companies are entering the atomistic world. Boundaries are blurring. Companies are aggressively verticalizing, and the market potential of these vertically integrated tech giants is far greater than anyone imagines for pure software companies. This leads to why the second point—too much money chasing too few companies—is wrong. Output is far greater than ever before, the software world is far more competitive, and companies are going public much later than ever before. All of this means that great companies simply need to raise far more money than ever before. Venture capital exists to invest in new markets. What we've learned time and again is that, in the long run, new markets are always far larger than we anticipate. The private market is mature enough to support top companies at unprecedented scale—just look at the liquidity available to top private firms today—and investors in both private and public markets now believe that the scale of venture capital output will be staggering. We have been misjudging how large VC as an asset class can and should be, and venture capital is scaling up to catch up with this reality, and the set of opportunities. The new world needs flying cars, a global satellite grid, abundant energy, and intelligence so cheap it's beyond measurement. The reality is that many of today's best companies are capital-intensive. OpenAI needs to spend billions of dollars on GPUs—more computing infrastructure than anyone imagines. Periodic Labs needs to build automated labs on an unprecedented scale for scientific innovation. Anduril needs to build a defensive future. And all of these companies need to recruit and retain the world's best talent in the most competitive talent market in history. The new generation of big winners—OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI, Anduril, Waymo, etc.—are all capital-intensive and have completed massive initial funding rounds at high valuations. Modern tech companies typically require hundreds of millions of dollars because the infrastructure needed to build world-changing cutting-edge technologies is simply too expensive. In the dot-com bubble, a "startup" entered an empty field, anticipating the needs of consumers who were still waiting for dial-up connections. Today, startups enter an economy shaped by three decades of tech giants. Supporting "Little Tech" means you have to be prepared to arm David against a few Goliaths. Companies in 2021 did indeed receive excessive funding, with a large portion of the money flowing into sales and marketing to sell products that weren't necessarily 10 times better. But today, the money is flowing into R&D or capital expenditures. Therefore, the winners are much larger than before and require significantly more funding, often from the outset. So, the venture capital industry naturally has to become much larger to meet this demand. Given the size of the opportunity set, this scaling is justified. If VC size were too large for the opportunities venture capitalists invest in, we should have seen the largest institutions underperform. But we haven't seen that at all. While expanding, top VC firms have repeatedly achieved extremely high multiples of returns—as have the LPs (limited partners) who can enter these firms. A famous venture capitalist once said that a $1 billion fund can never achieve a 3x return: because it's too big. Since then, some companies have exceeded 10x returns on a $1 billion fund. Some blame the asset class on underperforming institutions, but any industry that follows a power-law distribution has huge winners and a long tail of losers. The ability to win deals without relying on price is what allows institutions to maintain consistent returns. In other major asset classes, people sell products to or borrow from the highest bidder. But VC is a classic example of an asset class that competes on dimensions other than price. VC is the only asset class with significant consistency among the top 10% of institutions. The final point—that the VC industry should shrink—is also wrong. Or at least, it's bad for the tech ecosystem, for the goal of creating more generations of tech companies, and ultimately for the world. Some complain about the second-order effects of increased venture capital funding (and there are some!), but it's also been accompanied by a significant increase in startup valuations. Advocating for a smaller venture capital ecosystem could very well mean advocating for smaller startup valuations, and potentially resulting in slower economic growth. This perhaps explains why Garry Tan said in a recent podcast, "Venture capital can and should be 10 times bigger than it is now." Admittedly, if there were no competition and a particular LP or GP was the "only player," that might be good for them. But having more venture capital than we have today is clearly better for founders and for the world. To further illustrate this, let's consider a thought experiment. First, do you think there should be far more founders in the world than there are today? Second, if we suddenly had far more founders, what kind of institutions would best serve them? We're not going to spend too much time on the first question, because if you're reading this, you probably know we think the answer is obviously yes. We don't need to tell you too much about why founders are so good and so important. Great founders create great companies. Great companies create new products that improve the world, organize and direct our collective energy and risk appetite toward productive goals, and create disproportionate new enterprise value and interesting jobs around the world. And we could never have reached an equilibrium where everyone capable of starting a great company has already started one. That's why more venture capital helps unlock more growth in the startup ecosystem. But the second question is more interesting. If we woke up tomorrow to find the number of entrepreneurs 10 or 100 times greater than today (spoiler alert, this is happening), what would the world's startup scene look like? How should venture capital firms evolve in a more competitive world? To win, not lose everything. Marc Andreessen likes to tell a story about a famous venture capitalist who said the VC game is like a conveyor belt sushi restaurant: "A thousand startups come and go, you meet them. Then occasionally you reach out, pick a startup off the conveyor belt, and invest in it." The kind of VC Marc describes—well, that's how almost every VC has been for most of the last few decades. Back in the 1990s or 2000s, winning deals was that easy. Because of this, the only truly important skill for a great VC is judgment: the ability to distinguish between good and bad companies. Many VCs still operate this way—basically the same way VCs operated in 1995. But the world has changed dramatically. Winning a deal used to be easy—as easy as picking up sushi on a conveyor belt. Now it's incredibly difficult. VCs are sometimes described as poker: knowing when to pick a company, knowing at what price to enter, and so on. But that may mask the all-out war you have to wage to get the right to invest in the best companies. Old-school VCs miss the days when they were the "only player" and could dictate to founders. But now there are thousands of VC firms, and founders have an easier time getting term sheets than ever before. As a result, more and more of the best deals involve extremely fierce competition. The paradigm shift is that the ability to win a deal is becoming just as important as—or even more important than—picking the right companies. What's the point of picking the right deal if you can't get in? Several things have contributed to this change. First, the proliferation of venture capital firms means that VCs need to compete with each other to win deals. With more companies now competing for talent, customers, and market share than ever before, the best founders need strong institutional partners to help them win. They need institutions with resources, networks, and infrastructure to give their portfolio companies an edge. Secondly, because companies remain private for longer, investors can invest at later stages—when the companies are more validated and therefore more competitive in the deal—and still reap venture-style returns. Finally, and perhaps least obviously, the selection process has become slightly easier. The VC market has become more efficient. On one hand, there are more serial entrepreneurs constantly creating iconic companies. If Musk, Sam Altman, Palmer Luckey, or a brilliant serial entrepreneur starts a company, VCs will quickly line up to try and invest. On the other hand, companies reach insane size much faster (due to longer private periods and greater upside potential), so the risk of product-to-market fit (PMF) has decreased compared to the past. Finally, with so many great institutions now available, it's much easier for founders to connect with investors, making it harder to find deals that other institutions aren't pursuing. Selection remains central to the game—picking the right evergreen companies at the right price—but it's no longer the most crucial element. Ben Horowitz hypothesizes that consistently winning automatically makes you a top firm: because if you win, the best deals will come your way. You only have the right to pick when you can win any deal. You might not pick the right one, but at least you have the opportunity. Of course, if your firm consistently wins the best deals, you'll attract the best pickers to work for you because they want to work for the best firms. (As Martin Casado said when recruiting Matt Bornstein to a16z: "Come here to win deals, not lose deals.") Therefore, the ability to win creates a virtuous cycle that improves your pick-picking ability. For these reasons, the rules of the game have changed. My partner, David Haber, describes the shift venture capital needs to make to cope with this change in his article: "Firm > Fund." In my definition, a fund has only one objective function: "How can I generate the most carry (performance fees) with the fewest people and in the shortest amount of time?" A firm, in my definition, has two objectives. One is to deliver superior returns, but the second is equally interesting: "How can I build a source of competitive advantage that compoundes?" The best firms will be able to invest their management fees in strengthening their moats. How can I help? I entered the venture capital field a decade ago, and I quickly noticed that Y Combinator played a different game among all venture capital firms. YC was able to acquire great deals from excellent companies on a large scale, and at the same time, seemed to be able to serve them on a large scale. Many other VCs, compared to YC, played a commoditized game. I would go to Demo Day and think: I'm at the gambling table, and YC is the casino owner. We're all happy there, but YC is the happiest one. I quickly realized that YC has a moat. It has positive network effects. It has several structural advantages. People have said that venture capital firms can't have moats or unfair advantages—after all, you're just providing capital. But YC clearly has one. That's why YC remains so strong even after scaling up. Some critics don't like YC scaling up; they think YC will eventually fail because they feel it lacks soul. For the past 10 years, people have been predicting YC's demise. But it hasn't happened. In that time, they changed their entire partner team, and the death still didn't happen. A moat is a moat. Like the companies they invest in, the moat that a scaled venture capital firm possesses is more than just a brand. Then I realized I didn't want to play the same old VC game, so I co-founded my own firm, along with other strategic assets. These assets were incredibly valuable and generated a strong deal flow, so I got a taste of the differentiated game. Around the same time, I started watching another firm build its own moat: a16z. So when the opportunity to join a16z a few years later a few years later arose, I knew I had to seize it. If you believe in venture capital as an industry, you—almost by definition—believe in power-law distributions. But if you truly believe the VC game is governed by power laws, then you should believe that venture capital itself will also follow power laws. The best founders will flock to the firms that can most decisively help them win. The best returns will concentrate in those firms. Capital will follow. For founders trying to build the next iconic company, large-scale VC firms offer a highly attractive product. They offer expertise and a full range of services for everything a rapidly expanding company needs—recruiting, market entry strategy (GTM), legal, financial, public relations, government relations. They provide enough funding to get you to your destination, rather than forcing you to be frugal and struggle against well-funded competitors. They offer tremendous reach—access to everyone you need in the business and government sectors, introducing you to every major Fortune 500 CEO and every important world leader. They provide access to 100 times more talent, with a network of tens of thousands of top engineers, executives, and operators worldwide, ready to join your company whenever needed. And they are everywhere—which, for the most ambitious founders, means anywhere. Meanwhile, for LPs, scalable venture capital firms are also a highly attractive product on the most important and simple question: Are the companies driving the highest returns choosing them? The answer is simple—yes. All large companies are partnering with scalable platforms, usually at the earliest stages. Large-scale venture capital firms have more opportunities to swivel their batons to acquire key companies and more ammunition to persuade them to accept their investments. This is reflected in returns.

Excerpt from Packy's work: https://www.a16z.news/p/the-power-brokers

Think about where we are right now. Eight of the world's ten largest companies are West Coast-based, venture-backed firms. These firms have provided the majority of new enterprise value growth globally over the past few years. Meanwhile, the world's fastest-growing private companies are also predominantly West Coast-based venture-backed firms: those founded just a few years ago are rapidly heading towards trillion-dollar valuations and the largest IPOs in history. The best companies are winning more than ever before, and they all have the backing of large-scale institutions. Of course, not every large-scale institution performs well—I can think of some epic collapses—but almost every great tech company is backed by a large-scale institution. Either go big or go small. I don't think the future is just about large-scale venture capital firms. Like everything the internet has touched, venture capital will become a "barbell": at one end are a few very large players, and at the other end are many small, specialized institutions, each operating in a specific field and network, often in partnership with large-scale venture capital firms. What's happening in venture capital is exactly what usually happens when software eats up the services industry. At one end are four or five large, powerful players, often vertically integrated service providers; at the other end is a long tail of highly differentiated small vendors, built on the back of industry disruption. Both ends of the dumbbell will thrive: their strategies are complementary and mutually empowering. We also support hundreds of boutique fund managers outside of institutional investors and will continue to support and work closely with them.

Scaling and boutique will both thrive; the ones in the middle will struggle: these funds are too large to afford missing out on mega-winners, but too small to compete with larger institutions that can structurally offer better products for founders. a16z is unique in that it sits at both ends of the dumbbell—it's both a dedicated group of boutique institutions and benefits from a scalable platform team.

The institutions best positioned to work with founders will win. This could mean massive reserve funds, unprecedented reach, or a huge, complementary services platform. Or it could mean unreplicable expertise, excellent advisory services, or simply an incredible risk tolerance.

There's an old joke in the venture capital world: VCs believe every product can be improved, every great technology can be scaled, and every industry can be disrupted—except their own. In reality, many VCs don't like the existence of scaled venture capital firms at all. They believe scaling sacrifices some soul. Some say Silicon Valley is now too commercialized and no longer a haven for misfits. (Anyone claiming there aren't enough misfits in the tech world has clearly never been to a San Francisco tech party or listened to the MOTS podcast.) Others resort to a self-serving narrative—that change is "disrespectful to the game"—while ignoring that the game has always served the founders, and always has. Of course, they'll never express the same concerns about the companies they support, companies whose very existence is built on achieving massive scale and changing the game in their respective industries. Saying that large-scale venture capital firms aren't "real venture capital" is like saying that NBA teams shooting more three-pointers isn't playing "real basketball." You might not agree, but the old rules of the game no longer hold sway. The world has changed, and a new model has emerged. Ironically, the way the rules of the game are changing here is exactly the same way VC-backed startups are changing the rules of their industries. When technology disrupts an industry and a new batch of large-scale players emerges, there are always things lost in the process. But there are also many more gained. Venture capitalists understand this trade-off firsthand—they've always supported it. The disruptive process that venture capitalists hope to see in startups applies equally to venture capital itself. Software has devoured the world, and it certainly won't stop with VCs.

data.title: The Singularity Is Coming: When Encryption Rewrites the Underlying Logic of Digital Banks
data.description:
Author: Jay Yu; Translator: AididiaoJP, Foresight News

Introduction

Open any bank or fintech app, such as Bank of America, Revolut, Chase, SoFi, etc., and the interface looks almost identical: account, payment and transfer, earning returns.

This design reveals a commonality: banks are essentially interfaces representing the four basic relationships we have with money: Saving money: where assets are placed; Spending money: a means of daily payments; Earning money: a tool for managing wealth, including passive interest income and active transactions; Borrowing money: a channel for obtaining funds. Over the past decade, the mobile internet has spawned "digital banks" like SoFi, Revolut, and Wise. They've brought banks from physical branches into mobile phones, offering 24/7 online access and intuitive interfaces, making financial services no longer a privilege for a select few. Today, encryption technology has also been around for a decade. It is painting the next blueprint: self-custodial wallets, stablecoins, on-chain credit and yields—the permissionless and programmable nature of blockchain is giving rise to a global, instant, and freely combinable banking experience. If mobile technology created digital banks, then crypto technology is creating permissionless crypto banks: a unified, interoperable, and self-custodial front-end interface that allows you to save, spend, generate, and borrow money in the on-chain economy.

A Brief History of Fintech Digital Banks

Like crypto, digital banks also emerged after the 2008 financial crisis. They do not replicate physical branches, but instead provide banking services through mobile apps as technology companies. Most digital banks have partner banks providing deposit insurance and compliance support, while they themselves are responsible for the front-end customer experience. With fast account opening, transparent fees, and easy-to-use design, many digital banks have become the preferred entry point for ordinary people to save, spend, and generate money[1]. Looking back at those digital banks with market capitalizations of tens of billions of dollars, what they have in common is that they first used a unique digital function (student loan refinancing, early salary deposits, transparent exchange rates) to attract customers, allowing users to bring in transaction volume, forming a flywheel effect, and then gradually expanding product categories and charging value-added fees. Simply put—financial technology digital banks win by mastering the user's interaction interface with money, changing the way people save, spend, generate, and borrow money. Today's crypto industry is at a similar juncture to digital banks a decade ago. Over the past decade, crypto has already laid its own "wedge": using self-custodial wallets to achieve censorship-resistant asset storage; using stablecoins to create digital dollars that everyone can use; using protocols like Aave to create permissionless credit markets; and using 24/7 global capital markets to turn internet memes into wealth tools. Mobile infrastructure has enabled digital banks; programmable blockchains are now laying a permissionless financial foundation [2][3]. The next logical step is to connect these permissionless back-ends with the user-friendly front-ends of digital banks. The first wave of digital banks moved the bank's front-end from branches to mobile phones, while the bank's back-end remained unchanged; today's crypto digital banks do the opposite—the mobile front-end is retained, but how money is transferred and what path it takes has changed from the traditional banking track to stablecoins and public blockchains. In other words, digital banks have rebuilt the bank's front-end on the mobile track; crypto technology now gives us an opportunity to rebuild the bank's back-end on the permissionless track.

Map of Crypto Digital Banks

Map of Crypto Digital Bank Landscape

Today, a large number of projects are converging in the direction of "crypto digital banks". The most basic banking functions such as saving money, spending money, making money, and borrowing money are being realized one by one on the permissionless crypto track - you can use Ledger for self-custody, pay with Etherfi cards or Bitget QR codes, trade for value on Hyperliquid, and borrow money through Morpho. There is also a large circle of infrastructure service providers: wallet as a service, stablecoin settlement, licenses, localized deposit and withdrawal, routing scheduling, all are being laid out[26]. Even crypto exchanges like Binance and Coinbase have been making great strides toward digital banking, gradually taking over more of the relationship between users and their assets. Binance Pay already covers more than 20 million merchants worldwide [4]; Coinbase users can automatically earn up to 4% annualized return as long as they keep USDC on the platform. The map is a bit complicated and worth exploring carefully: What are these crypto platforms competing for? They are competing for the first layer of relationship between users and money—who do you think of first when you save money, spend money, make money, or borrow money?

Deposit Money in Crypto

To truly self-manage crypto assets and interact with the blockchain, you need a wallet. The crypto wallet landscape can be roughly divided into two parts: one is "security vs. ease of use," and the other is "consumer-grade vs. enterprise-grade." Each quadrant has produced winners with distribution capabilities—Ledger has captured the security-oriented consumer-grade hardware wallet market; Fireblocks and Anchorage have captured the security-oriented enterprise-grade wallet infrastructure market; MetaMask, Phantom, and Privy have focused on the ease of use of consumer-grade wallets; and Turnkey and Coinbase Prime have secured the ease of use of enterprise-grade infrastructure.

... Using wallets as a beachhead to build a digital bank has very direct advantages: wallets are the entry point. Front-end wallets like MetaMask and Phantom are the first door for users to enter the crypto world. The "fat wallet theory" states that the wallet layer consumes most of the distribution and order flow to consumers, and for end users, switching wallets is extremely costly [5]. Data also supports this point—today, about 35% of Solana's transaction volume flows through just one wallet, Phantom [6], which is a moat built on mobile experience and user stickiness. More realistically, consumers (especially retail investors) often prefer to spend more money to save time. Wallets like Phantom and MetaMask dare to charge 0.85% Swap fees [7], while the Uniswap protocol itself only charges 0.3% [8]. But conversely, it is very difficult to build an entire digital bank with just one wallet. The challenge lies in the fact that to achieve profitability, users can't simply leave their money idle. While Phantom, MetaMask, and Ledger are undoubtedly well-known, if users only treat their wallets as cash boxes under their beds, the brands won't monetize. Wallets must become trading platforms, encouraging frequent user activity to generate revenue. MetaMask and Phantom are both moving in this direction. MetaMask recently launched the MetaMask Card, targeting crypto natives, but with a clear intention—to capture the "how to spend crypto" scenario. Phantom followed suit, launching Phantom Cash and integrating Hyperliquid's Builder Code to enable perpetual contract trading directly within the app. Blockworks' comment hit the nail on the head: "Drift and Jupiter are local stars of Solana, but the money is all in Hyperliquid now." [9] Wallet players must remember this: it is not enough to just get the wallet, you also need to get the transaction volume of the wallet in and out - spending money, making money, borrowing money, you have to be in one of them. Spending money in a crypto way

Spending money in a crypto way

The second type of crypto digital bank players focus on "spending money in crypto". Like "saving money", we can also divide this map into two parts: one is "on-chain transfer vs off-chain shopping" (such as buying coffee); the other is "retail applications vs enterprise infrastructure". Interestingly, the new "digital bank" projects that have emerged in the past few months—Kast, Tria, Tempo, and Stable—are almost all focused on "spending money." Specifically, this wave of popularity is concentrated in two sub-tracks: (1) stablecoin card applications for retail users, such as Avici, Tria, Redotpay, and EtherFi; (2) "stablecoin-specific chains" for enterprises, such as Stable, Plasma, and Tempo. The first type of "retail spending applications" is making crypto apps increasingly resemble traditional bank apps—the homepage, bank, card, and investment tabs are almost identical. Crypto card issuers Rain and Reap have been operating for many years, and Visa and Mastercard's support for stablecoins is becoming increasingly mature. Today, issuing a crypto card itself has no major barriers and has become a standardized service. The real differentiation lies in building and retaining transaction volume—is it through innovative cashback mechanisms, a regional "ground war" strategy, or attracting non-crypto users? This scenario mirrors the early days of fintech digital banks: the winner isn't the first to launch a card or app, but the first to capture a specific demographic—students (SoFi), low-income families (Chime), international travelers (Wise, Revolut)—and then grow through trust and transaction volume. If done correctly, these payment-first digital banks could become crucial entry points for the large-scale deployment of blockchain infrastructure. Of course, crypto digital banks also have the opportunity to directly propel users to the next generation of payment systems. Card payments are still a transitional solution—Visa and Mastercard remain centralized. The next generation is already showing signs: Bitget Wallet has piloted merchant QR code and stablecoin payment solutions in Indonesia, Brazil, and Vietnam. Crypto-native settlements, completely bypassing card issuers, are possible. The second type of "digital bank" application is enterprise-oriented stablecoin infrastructure projects, such as Stable, Plasma, Tempo, and Arc. They are often called "stablecoin chains". Their rise in this round is largely due to institutional players - traditional banks, financial technology companies like Stripe, and established payment networks - starting to seriously want more efficient cross-border transfer tools. The design patterns of these "stablecoin chains" are also becoming more and more similar [10]: Gas fees are paid directly with stablecoins, saving the trouble of token price fluctuations; the consensus mechanism is simplified and optimized for simple transfers with high concurrency; privacy protection is added to transfers using technologies such as TEE; and data fields are compatible with international payment standards such as ISO 20022 [11]. However, technological upgrades do not guarantee that people will use them. For payment chains, the moat is merchants. The key is how many merchants and how many enterprises can be persuaded to move their real business onto this chain. Tempo's approach is straightforward: leveraging Stripe's massive merchant base, they directly switch their payment track to their own [12], bringing a whole new customer base into crypto payments. Plasma and Stable want to take a different path—becoming "first-class citizens" for Tether USDT to circulate among institutions. The most noteworthy case study in this sector is actually Tron. 25%-30% of global stablecoin transactions are on Tron [13]. Why? Not because it has the strongest technology, but because it first captured emerging markets—Nigeria, Argentina, Brazil, and Southeast Asia. With low fees, fast settlement, and global usability, Tron has become the underlying platform for countless merchants' payments, cross-border remittances, and even USD deposit accounts [14]. All new payment chains that want to beat Tron must have a 10x advantage in a sector that is already cheap, fast, and has global coverage. This is not something that can be achieved by a few technical parameters; what truly matters is the merchant network and economies of scale. The third type of relationship between crypto banks and users is "helping users make money." This is also the area with the highest density of innovation in the crypto industry—staking vaults, perpetual contracts, token issuance platforms, prediction markets—several inventions from scratch have all come from here. Like the previous examples, this map can also be divided into two parts: passive interest collection vs. active trading, front-end interface vs. back-end liquidity. The most classic path for a "money-making platform" to grow into a "digital bank" is through centralized exchanges. Binance and Coinbase started with a simple but effective value proposition: if you want to make money through crypto, trade here. After the trading volume grew, exchanges became not only places to make money, but also places to put money[15]. Then both companies developed their own chains, wallets, institutional products, and crypto cards—the core user group remained the same, but the product line was extended, and the ARPU increased. Binance Pay is now being used by more and more small merchants to collect daily payments. DeFi projects have also followed the same script. EtherFi started as an Ethereum liquidity staking protocol, helping users stake ETH on EigenLayer to earn passive income[16]. Then they created "Liquid," an actively managed DeFi strategy vault that pursues higher returns. Then came EtherFi Cash—a credit card that allows users to use their EtherFi balance in the real world. This expansion route is exactly the same as that of financial technology digital banks: first, use a unique function (passive staking, earning interest) to gain a foothold, become a leader in a niche category, attract trading volume, and then expand the category and collect value-added fees (EtherFi card).

There are already many "money-making" innovations emerging in the crypto field[27] - perpetual contract platforms like Hyperliquid are already among the most profitable companies in the industry; prediction markets like Polymarket have also entered the mainstream. These platforms will almost certainly do the same thing next: expand their product categories, allow users to spend and save more money in their own ecosystem, and monetize the network scale.

Starting with "money-making platforms" (especially active trading platforms) has a natural advantage: high trading frequency and large turnover. Hyperliquid has traded $3 trillion in the past 18 months[17]. Compared to "saving platforms" and "spending platforms", the user flywheel of "money-making platforms" turns much faster and the migration cost is much higher - the transition from the main trading account to saving and spending money is a natural progression. But these platforms also have hidden worries: they are too dependent on market cycles, and it is difficult to shake off the label of "financial casino". Ordinary people have different mindsets when it comes to banks and casinos. Borrowing money in a crypto way is like the traditional economy; access to loans is a crucial fuel for on-chain economic growth. For crypto banks, lending is also one of the most sustainable sources of revenue. Borrowing in the traditional world is highly "permissioned"—you need to pass KYC, provide a credit report, and prove your repayment history. Crypto lending is different: permissioned and permissionless lending coexist, and the requirements for collateral are completely different.
