Today, the mainstream of crypto lending is still permissionless, with on-chain over-collateralization. DeFi giants like Aave, Morpho, and Sky (formerly MakerDAO) believe in "code is law." If the blockchain can't get your FICO points and there's no past credit history to check, then over-collateralization is used to guarantee repayment ability[22]—sacrificing capital efficiency in exchange for barrier-free access and a safety cushion against default. Morpho represents a new generation of evolution of this type of model[23]: modular, permissionless, more refined risk pricing, and higher capital efficiency.

The other end is permissioned lending. After institutional capital started entering DeFi, this type of demand became obvious—for example, market makers need to borrow money. Maple Finance, Goldfinch, and Clearpool all target this institutional customer group, essentially building a traditional credit front end on the chain. Under-collateralization is possible, at the cost of KYC, off-chain legal agreements, and whitelist access[24]. The moat of this type of protocol is not only in liquidity (that's more like the logic of permissionless pools), but also in compliance framework and B2B sales capabilities. There is another type of player in permissioned lending, such as Figure Markets, Nexo, and Coinbase Lending, which take the compliance-first route and target retail customers. They both conduct KYC and require over-collateralization, and some even wrap Morpho as the backend (such as Coinbase Lending[25]). The core selling point of these products is that they are faster than traditional banks in lending. But the real "holy grail" of crypto lending is under-collateralized consumer credit—this is the same entry point that SoFi, Chime and other fintech digital banks used to "provide banking services to the unbanked." Crypto has not yet truly mastered this aspect and has failed to replicate the consumer credit flywheel of fintech digital banks. The core reason is that two things are missing: digital identities resistant to Sybil attacks and on-chain consequences of default. The only pure crypto-native exception is flash loans—temporary uncollateralized loans that utilize the characteristics of blockchain mechanisms—but flash loans are still mainly tools for arbitrage bots and complex strategies, not for ordinary people. If the next generation of crypto banks succeeds, they will most likely stand in the middle of this map—combining the speed and transparency of permissionless DeFi with the capital efficiency of traditional lending. The key to victory may lie in who can create a decentralized identity layer, or commoditize it, thereby unlocking consumer credit and allowing crypto to truly rebuild the underlying mechanism of a credit card. Until then, crypto banks may have to continue relying on over-collateralized loans to feed DeFi yields. The fundamental value proposition of crypto banks, in essence, boils down to one sentence: making money move faster—this is exactly what SoFi and Chime did with mobile apps a decade ago. The essence of the blockchain is to compress the distance between any two accounts into a single transaction, eliminating the need to navigate through layers of international banks, SWIFT, and countless unseen legacy systems. Saving, spending, generating, and borrowing money—each monetary relationship benefits from this "flattening dividend" in different ways, with varying monetization models and trade-offs. But when you stack these models together, I increasingly feel they form a pyramid, ordered by the velocity of money. At the top is "generating money," where the velocity of money is fastest (e.g., Hyperliquid's transaction fees), followed by "borrowing money" (monetizing through interest), then "spending money" (relying on exchange fees and foreign exchange spreads), and at the bottom is "saving money" (relying on deposit and withdrawal fees and enterprise integration). From this perspective, the easiest path to becoming a cryptocurrency bank might be to start from the top of the pyramid—beginning with the highest capital flow and most frequent user participation, such as generating and lending money. First, capture the value in motion, then move down the pyramid, converting existing users into full-category financial customers. The Opportunities for Cryptocurrency Banks So, where is the next step for cryptocurrency banks? Which directions can give rise to the next generation of permissionless digital banks? I think there are at least five intertwined paths worth exploring further: 1 - Privacy and Compliance: Catching Up on Equivalence Stablecoins and crypto tracks are faster and easier to use than traditional tracks; this is already a consensus. However, for crypto digital banks to compete head-on with fintech pioneers and existing banking systems, there are two dimensions they must catch up on to achieve functional parity: privacy and compliance. Retail scenarios may not care much about privacy—stablecoins have grown so large without relying on privacy protection as a foundation. But enterprise scenarios are different: once payroll, supply chain financing, and cross-border settlements are on-chain, transactions are public, and trade secrets and sensitive information are readily available [18]. Why do almost all the newly launched "stablecoin chains" include privacy in their roadmaps? This is part of the reason. On the other hand, there is compliance. For crypto digital banks to truly grow, regulatory barriers and licensing systems must be built step by step. Consumers and merchants need to feel that using crypto solutions is as compliant as traditional solutions—or even smarter through new technologies like zero-knowledge proofs. Only by addressing both enterprise-level privacy and enterprise-level compliance can crypto digital banks truly overcome the hurdle posed by fintech pioneers.

2 - Composability in the Real World

Composability has always been a hallmark of the crypto space—universal standards, smart contracts, Lego bricks. But today, this "composability" is mostly confined to the crypto sphere: combining with this DeFi primitive, combining with that yield protocol, combining across several EVM chains. The real challenge is: bridging blockchain standards with legacy standards from various eras in the real world—international banking systems like SWIFT, merchant POS systems, ISO 20022 messaging standards, ACH, Pix… This path is gradually being opened up as crypto cards become more widespread and stablecoin cross-border payments increase.

Another issue is the user base. Today, most crypto card products are essentially still withdrawal tools serving crypto natives, earning tolls from "crypto whales" going global.

The real challenge for cryptocurrency banks is whether they can transcend the traditional user base and persuade those unfamiliar with crypto to bring their real-world business to these platforms—provided that real-world composability is sufficiently robust and the deposit and withdrawal experience is smooth enough.

3 - Utilizing the "Permissionless" Card

Cryptobanks, in essence, are reinventing a more efficient monetary standard: instantaneous, globally liquid, infinitely programmable, and not subject to restrictions from any single institution or government. Today, anyone with a crypto wallet can open a contract on Hyperliquid, send a USDC cross-border transfer, or deposit ETH into an EtherFi vault to earn returns—all without the need for approval from fiat currency authorities. This permissionless underlying mechanism is a card that cryptocurrency banks must utilize effectively.

The logic of the crypto track is: global capital flows at internet speed, and the coordination mechanism is based on incentives and game theory, not fiat currency directives.

The next generation of digital banks aims to rapidly integrate this permissionless capability into the existing financial system—allowing new primitives like perpetual contracts, prediction markets, staking, and token issuance to be freely combined with traditional services such as bank cards, remittances, and credit cards. Another possibility is that in economies with high stablecoin penetration, a permissionless card network could emerge, similar to alternatives to Visa or Mastercard. The logic is the opposite of today's crypto cards—instead of using fiat acquiring banks to peer-to-peer convert stablecoin withdrawals into fiat currency, it uses native crypto acquiring banks for default on-chain settlement. Of course, to be compatible with traditional payment tools (such as fiat credit cards), this system must also be able to record fiat currency payments on-chain and convert them into stablecoins. Permissionlessness isn't just for human users. AI agents may be an even bigger variable—for AI agents, opening a crypto wallet is far simpler than opening a bank account. With stablecoins, AI agents can initiate on-chain transactions themselves with user authorization or pre-set strategies. I've already written about agent payment standards like Coinbase's x402. What new e-commerce models they can develop is still in its early stages. Permissionless digital banks form the underlying foundation of this system and the front-end interface of the human-computer interaction economy—AI agents can be your wealth manager, shopping assistant, or even help you secure credit lines.

4 - Localization or Globalization

Crypto digital banks need to choose sides: depth or breadth.

One ​​approach is to follow Nubank's path: first capture a region, then expand outwards through deep localization, cultural adaptation, and regulatory breakthroughs—remittance corridors (US/EU/UAE↔ India/Latin America/Southeast Asia) and supply chains (EU/US/Latin America↔ China/Hong Kong) are all viable paths.

Another approach is global priority: products are license-free and promoted globally. Wherever the network effect is fast, we invest there. Both paths can be successful. The former wins through local trust and channels, while the latter wins through scale and composability. Stablecoins are the highway for international payments, but crypto digital banks still need local outlets—Pix, UPI, Alipay, VietQR—to deeply integrate with these regional payment systems so that local people and merchants can use them. Crypto native digital banks also have a unique entry point: "providing banking services for the unbanked." In regions where financial infrastructure is lacking and local currencies are depreciating, Argentina's hyperinflation has boosted crypto adoption rates[19]—dollar-denominated (or crypto-denominated) capital accounts are a necessity. The future scenario may be: regional super apps and globally composable digital banks coexist, each using its own methods to reap the benefits of the permissionless underlying infrastructure.

5 - Undercollateralized Mortgages and Consumer Credit

Undercollateralized mortgages and consumer credit—this is the "holy grail" of crypto-banks. All the challenges mentioned earlier converge here.

First, compliance and KYC: To implement undercollateralization, a reliable identity system resistant to Sybil attacks is essential. The overcollateralization logic of Aave, Morpho, and MakerDAO won't work. This identity mechanism could be biometric verification like Worldcoin, or it could be a zero-knowledge proof-based solution, such as DECO. Second, it's necessary to bridge off-chain credit profiles with on-chain identities (like what 3Jane is doing), and ensure that on-chain defaults affect off-chain credit records. Different countries have different credit models, data standards, and legal frameworks, making compatibility with legacy systems even more difficult.

This is why today's under-collateralized loans in DeFi are almost all concentrated in institutional private lending (Maple Finance, Goldfinch)[20][21], rather than consumer credit—although the latter is several orders of magnitude larger in the traditional financial world. Perhaps the solution lies not only in the identity layer, but also in the mechanism design. Flash loans are a purely crypto-native example—temporary uncollateralized loans achieved through blockchain characteristics. Along this direction, perhaps smarter collateral management can be used to reduce the collateral requirements for on-chain loans: for example, setting revolving credit limits for stablecoins and interest-bearing collateral (LST/LRT), real-time monitoring of LTV, automated liquidation buffers, and automatic repayment using staking yields. These technical paths have not yet been fully explored. If this path proves successful—with under-collateralized loans and consumer credit functioning on-chain—it will significantly increase the velocity of money circulation on-chain, giving the unbanked a real reason to be on-chain and propelling the on-chain economy to the next level. Just like under-collateralized loans in the real world have always been an engine of economic growth. Conclusion: Ten years ago, fintech digital banks reinvented how we deal with money; today, crypto-banks stand at the same crossroads. The difference lies in the front end: fintech digital banks changed the front end—putting branches into mobile phones—while the back end remained the FDIC banking partnership model. Crypto-banks, on the other hand, need to change the back end: using stablecoins and public blockchains to build a global, composable, censorship-resistant underlying network for fund transfers. Crypto-bank apps are no longer just interfaces; they could be gateways to a new generation of programmable financial systems. But this is just the beginning. Building a full-stack crypto digital bank isn't just about issuing a crypto card or creating a wallet with a UI. Like fintech digital banks, you must first clearly define your entry point—is it "providing banking services to the unbanked" or "enabling merchants in emerging markets to scan QR codes to receive stablecoins"?—and then quickly extend your product line and thoroughly understand your target audience. Saving, spending, generating, and lending money—each vertical has different monetization models and trade-offs, but the real big revenue comes from value in motion. From this perspective, the biggest opportunity for crypto digital banks may not lie in the slow money areas of saving and spending, but in the areas where money flows fastest—generating and lending money. First, secure your position in the high-flow-rate area, then move down the money flow pyramid. The challenges of privacy and compliance, real-world composability, the release of permissionless capabilities, the trade-offs between local and global paths, and breakthroughs in under-collateralized consumer credit—as crypto-banks unravel these issues one by one, they will gradually transform from niche entry points for crypto assets into the default operating system of the global economy. The first wave of digital banks used mobile technology to change the banking interface; this wave is rewriting the underlying logic of currency itself.

data.title: New regulations for crypto assets in China take effect: RWA is specifically mentioned. What can it still do?
data.description:
After the release of two documents regarding cryptocurrencies a couple of days ago, my social media feed was flooded with them. I saw many colleagues writing articles to interpret them, and short videos discussing RWA (Real-Time Tokenization) became very popular.

In the first half, everyone was focused on the fact that the entire crypto world was about to tighten, collapse, or even crumble; then, China celebrated the Lunar New Year, with some saying that China could finally issue its own cryptocurrency, creating a lively atmosphere.

Let me share my basic viewpoint: Strictly speaking, there is indeed a clear contraction. Many crypto assets or Web3-related businesses that were previously operating in the cracks in China are now facing significant challenges to their survival, such as mining. I also mentioned that China has finally left a loophole in the RWA area. Of course, this statement may not be entirely accurate, because according to the China Securities Regulatory Commission (CSRC), it is not actually RWA, but rather called security tokenization. However, from an industry perspective, security tokenization is one type within the broader RWA framework we are discussing.

So I think we don't need to be too rigid about the literal meaning; it's enough that everyone understands what the China Securities Regulatory Commission (CSRC) is trying to convey. Some reporters have also interviewed me about my personal views on these two documents. In the previous short video, I mainly talked about RWA (Regulatory Advisory Board), but I personally believe there are still many aspects of this regulatory document that can be interpreted. Therefore, in this short video, I will spend more time discussing my views from my perspective, based on the relevant content of the two documents. More importantly, for those of us working in this industry, and based on the business practices I've observed, can we still operate in mainland China? If not, what should we do? This is probably what I want to discuss in this short video. This video might be a little long, so if you're really interested in this topic, I suggest you save it for later. First, we can look at the relevant topic of cryptocurrency risk prevention and handling issued by the People's Bank of China and eight other ministries, as reported by the *Daily Economic News*. We can examine its points one by one. The first interesting point is that it directly defines the tokenization of real-world assets at the outset, which is quite subtle. Those familiar with Chinese regulations on crypto assets know that much has already been said, such as virtual currencies being equivalent to fiat currencies and lacking legal protection. However, this is the first time Chinese regulators have explicitly defined the tokenization of real-world assets at the beginning of an article or regulatory document. So this is quite strange; from a Web3 perspective, RWA may simply be a commercial sector, and its global application is not as good as imagined. In many discussions, I've mentioned that there are different types of RWAs globally. The first type, exemplified by the US financial market, involves tokenizing traditional financial assets. A prime example is US stocks. Many exchanges, whether full spot or full derivatives exchanges, are now promoting this, allowing users on cryptocurrency trading platforms to not only buy and sell traditional crypto assets but also a significant amount of US stocks. This is a relatively promising area. There are many people globally who want access to US capital market assets but lack access through traditional securities accounts. This is a common area or direction, and I personally have high hopes for it. The second type is within the Hong Kong framework, as seen in companies like Ant Financial, Langxin, GCL, and Xunying since the year before last. These business service providers sell assets in Hong Kong to qualified investors or investment institutions within mainland China. Of course, this only exists in the primary market and hasn't created liquidity in the secondary market. Thirdly, from a financial risk prevention perspective, Chinese regulators should be most concerned about the large amount of mainland assets, funds, and audiences active in China, using tokens similar to NFTs, digital collectibles, or those issued under the name RWA. This third category is precisely what regulators should focus on preventing. Therefore, after the regulatory documents were issued, I saw many people in the RWA field on social media platforms and short video platforms cheering, saying they were ready to regroup and that this day had finally come. Personally, I think this is premature. Because the methods and practices these people or teams used to operate fall under the category of illegal financial activities explicitly defined in the regulatory documents. The fact that this regulatory document places RWA (Real Asset Tokenization) at the very beginning indicates that, from a financial risk prevention perspective, it primarily targets a third group of people, making their enthusiasm all the more intriguing. Therefore, Attorney Hong Lin strongly advises everyone to be extremely cautious when conducting any RWA-related activities in mainland China, whether it's providing comprehensive business consulting, offering so-called token issuance technology solutions, conducting market promotion, or directly selling products to end users. This carries enormous risks in China. Placing it at the beginning of the document certainly has significant value and reasons. There's also the issue of inter-departmental collaboration. I've previously discussed why there will be two documents defining stablecoins and RWA by the end of 2025. The core reason is that Chinese regulatory authorities need to reach a consensus in the field of crypto assets and blockchain. From a judicial practice perspective, the legal treatment of the entire crypto world is currently fragmented: from a civil law perspective, it belongs to the private property of citizens and should be protected; however, the views and understandings of courts and government departments in different regions may be inconsistent. The same situation may be protected in Shanghai, but not in some other inland cities. Therefore, this requires coordination among multiple departments. The core point of this document is that the procuratorate, courts, the Cyberspace Administration of China, and financial institutions all need to form a collaborative mechanism. This means that in cases or projects involving crypto assets and blockchain, all departments need to maintain consistency in their statements and actions. Taking another document No. 1 issued by the China Securities Regulatory Commission as an example, besides understanding related products as part of the securities regulatory framework, it also requires compliance with other relevant requirements from the Cyberspace Administration of China, financial regulatory departments, etc., which can be considered a cautious innovation. Strengthening risk prevention departments also brings the State Administration of Foreign Exchange into the picture. Because we know that as the year draws to a close, especially after the regulatory definition of stablecoins, a very important regulatory direction is actually regarding OTC merchants. I have been strongly advising some of my partners who previously did OTC business to really slow down recently. This is because the core focus of domestic attention on cryptocurrencies has shifted from the original "issuing tokens and ICOs to raise funds" to "being wary of raising funds through RWA." This is actually a key point regarding the prevention of illegal fundraising and financial risks. Furthermore, one of the most concerning issues for regulators is capital outflow. Since traditional methods may be subject to restrictions, many partners are hoping to use stablecoins like USDT to transfer funds across borders. From the regulators' perspective, this is also a key area of ​​focus. Moreover, we understand that since last year, there have been related projects or research directions that aim to use on-chain tracking combined with off-chain interbank fund flows to conduct big data screening and monitoring, thereby identifying the paths of related capital outflows. Therefore, we have always conservatively believed that mainland China will not actually open up to cryptocurrencies. A crucial point is the issue of capital management systems, which is something everyone in the industry needs to focus on. From this perspective, whether you are a narrowly defined OTC merchant, or someone with related business needs who facilitates matching and facilitating transactions, or someone who uses exchanges to guide users to buy crypto assets and then transfers or uses them for other purposes, the risk is too high, and we strongly advise against it. At the same time, we also see some restrictions on intermediaries and related service groups. I think this is quite business-oriented, because the reason this regulatory document is so detailed is because it focuses on and describes many micro-level scenarios within the industry. For example, it even mentions that: virtual currencies and related financial products cannot be included in the scope of collateral or pledges; and that insurance businesses related to virtual currencies cannot be conducted, etc. I don't know how everyone will understand this statement. Because we know that some people in the OTC market are now using a different phrasing: "I'm lending you money, but I'll use cryptocurrency as collateral." I don't know if everyone understands this meaning. In traditional trading, it's a cash-on-delivery transaction, which falls under the category of cryptocurrency trading. But now some people are getting "savvy," and they might want to make an adjustment: "I'm lending you money, a pure lending relationship, but I'm worried about your repayment risk. You happen to have some economically valuable cryptocurrency assets, and you provide them to me as collateral through pledging or other means. I'm not helping you exchange cryptocurrency for fiat currency; it's actually a lending relationship." This is a very subtle scenario. But can you imagine? A description like this is actually included directly in such regulatory documents. So from this perspective, we can understand to some extent that regulatory authorities are quite familiar with the entire cryptocurrency industry, especially with some of the tricks and tactics used in mainland China. Conversely, this is also because many insiders or industry partners describe and explain many business details when facing regulatory authorities. So don't think that just because you're being clever, the relevant departments won't understand or see your tricks; that's highly unlikely. I think this document does have many points that can be discussed and studied in detail, which is why I'm using a relatively long short video to talk about this. The seventh point is "strengthening the management of internet information content and access." Everyone should understand this point, because currently on many short video media platforms, such as Douyin, Douyin, and WeChat Video, we see many overseas exchanges or project teams using a large number of "alias" accounts to promote themselves, and even recruit. The platforms will also, according to the requirements of the Cyberspace Administration of China, regularly tighten restrictions on some sensitive words and control related accounts. Regulatory authorities have already noticed this trend, and therefore, the regulatory requirements for these internet platforms are becoming increasingly stringent. When law enforcement collaboration is involved, relevant internet platforms or content platforms also need to cooperate. The eighth point is "strengthening the registration and advertising management of business entities." Here, I'd like to discuss two details. The first detail concerns Khorgos in Xinjiang, where there is a free trade zone. From what I understand, a couple of years ago, when the local management committee issued business licenses to some companies within its jurisdiction, the scope of business included "judicial disposal of cryptocurrencies." Therefore, such regulations or documents specifically target this type of detail. Many free trade zones might think they can pilot some financial innovations within their zones. However, this document actually directly expresses a negative attitude. Another small detail is that we know last year, some investment products related to cryptocurrencies appeared on a certain platform. Of course, it sold out on the same day and was then taken down. That financial product was essentially an investment fund. If I remember correctly, about 10% of its underlying assets were invested in Bitcoin ETFs and some crypto-asset stocks, such as Coinbase shares. So strictly speaking, it wasn't a cryptocurrency fund product as we understand it. However, advertisements for investing in related assets through this multi-layered nesting method are also subject to regulatory restrictions. Therefore, it was also taken down and cannot be advertised or promoted. The ninth point concerns the crackdown on cryptocurrency mining activities. A particularly important point here is that, in addition to the emphasis since 2021 that any cryptocurrency mining discovered in mainland China must be shut down and no new mining is allowed, this is also a response to the resurgence of cryptocurrency mining activities in mainland China over the past year. We know that mainland China once accounted for 70% of the global Bitcoin hashrate, with very high output. However, after 2021, theoretically, all of China's cryptocurrency mining has moved overseas. However, according to lawyer Hong Lin's observations over the past two years, from a statistical perspective, China still possesses at least 20% of the computing power in Bitcoin mining. In the past six months, I've seen many people directly selling mining machines and even providing mining machine hosting services in news media, such as on social media platforms like Douyin, and in my WeChat Moments. Of course, these things are often well-hidden, for example, within cloud computing centers and computing power centers in various locations. Some may be more blatant, thinking that the government's attitude on this issue has softened. But in reality, this is a reaffirmation and emphasis: it's not allowed. Another point I think everyone needs to pay special attention to is: mining machine manufacturers are strictly prohibited from providing mining machine sales and other services within China. This is essentially saying that mining is prohibited, and selling mining machines within the mainland is also prohibited. Because I often see people selling mining machines on WeChat Moments. This essentially means that mining machines can only be exported from now on, not sold in mainland China. Those selling mining machines, hardware, or so-called "nodes" or "servers" under the guise of "DePin" on WeChat Moments need to pay special attention. There's a misconception that selling mining machines for other minor cryptocurrencies is acceptable, as long as it's not Bitcoin mining. However, regulators are aware of this and are cracking down on such borderline activities. Later, it will be mentioned that besides illegal financial activities, if criminal offenses are involved, the case will be transferred to the police; we won't elaborate on that. Strengthening industry self-regulation will also be discussed in detail. The fourth part, which is the main focus, concerns RWA, etc. This document clearly defines that if you are a domestic entity and want to conduct related activities in mainland China under the name of RWA, this is not allowed. If you are a domestic company or project and want to issue related RW tokens directly overseas, it's not possible. However, there is a small loophole: if you have obtained approval or filing with the relevant regulatory authorities, you can then conduct related operations overseas. This echoes or corresponds to the China Securities Regulatory Commission's (CSRC) Document No. 1 of 2026. This document states that if a domestic entity or project wants to issue RWA products overseas, firstly, you definitely need to file with the CSRC in China. While it's not an approval process, I think its authority is slightly weaker, but the threshold and requirements are still quite high. Secondly, if you want to issue RWA products overseas, you must find an overseas subsidiary or branch of a domestic financial institution. There are also requirements regarding some business details, but I understand it's basically the same logic and standards as overseas bond issuance. Therefore, these points clearly define the boundaries of what is permissible and impermissible in the Web3 industry's understanding of the RWA field. The restrictions are as follows: If the assets are domestic, you cannot directly issue related tokens within China; if the assets are domestic, when issuing RWA tokens overseas, you cannot target mainland Chinese consumers; even if your project and assets are overseas, you cannot sell them to mainland Chinese residents. The restrictions are as follows: After filing with the China Securities Regulatory Commission (CSRC) in China, you can find overseas subsidiaries or branches of domestic financial institutions to provide the relevant issuance services. Furthermore, all information regarding the entire product and project operation process must be promptly shared with the regulatory authorities. This is essentially what we see as what is permissible and impermissible in the RWA field. Therefore, the CSRC's Document No. 1 describes some negative lists (i.e., what cannot be done, which companies cannot do, and which individuals cannot do). Although its wording and expression do not use the term "RWA," I understand this to be a cautious innovation. This morning, a reporter interviewed me, asking for my views on this matter. I believe that, under the current circumstances, China's efforts to address the issue of high-quality assets on the mainland, or rather, the development direction the country wants to encourage, can be achieved through securities tokenization. On the one hand, this can support Hong Kong's financial innovation and industrial applications in Web3. On the other hand, it can be considered a cautious financial innovation under the supervision of the China Securities Regulatory Commission (CSRC). However, it may be very far removed from what our industry partners want to achieve. This is basically not something small players or startups can do. Therefore, I think that things like what Ant Group has been doing before will likely involve collaboration with the CSRC, and it may be among the first in China to implement and launch applications in this direction. Therefore, I believe that either a large enough internet company with fintech attributes can do this, or some listed companies in China might allocate some projects for pilot projects. The initial compliance process and costs are actually very high. So I think if you're concerned about RWA, you can hold off for another six months or a year and see how things develop. Finally, regarding legal liability, this also addresses a concern many have raised since this news came out. One point is, what are the legal risks for individual investors investing in crypto assets or related financial products? This point isn't new; it remains the same: civil acts are invalid, and the risk is borne by the individual. Another point is that those suspected of disrupting financial order or endangering financial security will be investigated and dealt with by relevant departments according to law. There's a slightly subtle point here: "financial order" actually includes systems like foreign exchange management. This means that if you buy cryptocurrency purely for personal cross-border use, there will still be significant legal risks if discovered. Everyone should understand this, so we'll leave it at that. Besides individual players, we also know that a large number of KOLs and Chinese-speaking operational partners are actually active in mainland China. A friend told me that 40% of global cryptocurrency trading volume is currently conducted in mainland China. The exchanges that constantly talk about compliance, apart from the two in Hong Kong, mostly still conduct business face-to-face, a practice known as "selective compliance": applying for licenses overseas while simultaneously conducting similar activities in China. We won't judge this now, but what I really want to emphasize is that KOLs or content creators providing traffic and promotion services for these institutions should pay attention. I mentioned at an event recently that many partners feel it's not a big problem to post rebate links on foreign media like Twitter (formerly Twitter) to get users to register and receive rebates. However, in reality, the content and information we publish in foreign media will be noticed, and everyone should understand what that means. Therefore, for those of us in mainland China, whether through activities or links, helping some non-compliant overseas exchanges (here, "compliant" is a gray area term) to attract customers is a matter of probability and luck if it's a large, core platform; but if it's a smaller platform, people are attracted by its high commissions and promote it extensively. If, during subsequent law enforcement, the platform is found to be operating illegally, or even involved in gambling-related content, the criminal risk for the person attracting the traffic is very high. Therefore, regarding legal liability, in addition to focusing on individual holdings and investments, we also need to pay close attention to the partners who attract and promote these trading institutions or OTC merchants. This is something we particularly need to focus on. That concludes my nearly half-hour discussion of this regulatory document, along with the CSRC's Document No. 1, and some of my personal views. Of course, I didn't prepare a written transcript, so the expression may not be entirely precise, but I believe the core points and meanings have been conveyed. I hope this will be of some positive help to everyone's future business choices, overseas expansion strategies, or personal investments and transactions. For more detailed questions, please add me on WeChat for further discussion.

data.title: Binance Controls 87% of Trump-Backed USD1 as Stablecoin Surges to $5.4B
data.description:
Binance Controls 87% of Trump-Backed USD1 Stablecoin as Political and Market Stakes Rise

Binance now custodies a staggering 87% of the total supply of USD1, the flagship stablecoin issued by Trump family-backed World Liberty Financial (WLF), according to blockchain intelligence firm Arkham.

Out of USD1’s $5.4 billion circulating supply, approximately $4.7 billion is held in Binance-linked wallets — an unprecedented level of concentration among major global stablecoins and one that is rapidly drawing scrutiny across both financial and political circles.

Binance Controls 87% of Trump-Linked Stablecoin #USD1 🚨

Forbes reveals Binance is holding ~$4.7B of USD1, accounting for nearly 87% of the $5.4B circulating supply

its one of the highest single-exchange concentrations ever seen in a major stablecoin

$USD1 is issued by World… pic.twitter.com/RCK0az6lvU

— 🦧Mr. APE aka GEM Hunter💎 (@deg_ape) February 11, 2026

The custody dominance comes amid explosive growth, with USD1’s market capitalization surging 50% since January, transforming the token into one of the fastest-scaling stablecoins in the market.

USD1’s rapid expansion has been fueled by aggressive strategic moves. A $40 million WLFI token airdrop incentivized Binance users to hold the stablecoin on the exchange, while a landmark $2 billion investment by Abu Dhabi-backed MGX in May 2025 — executed in USD1 — cemented Binance as the ecosystem’s primary liquidity hub.

If you still think crypto is a fad, explain this:

MGX just put $2 billion into Binance—the biggest investment in crypto EVER.

Why? Because:

- AI + Blockchain = The financial revolution
- Binance dominates the industry
- Institutional money isn’t here to gamble—it’s here to… pic.twitter.com/pGoRvGA6cb

— Ak47♛ (@HolaItsAk47) March 13, 2025

The financial architecture behind the project adds another layer of intrigue. A Trump-affiliated entity reportedly owns 38% of World Liberty Financial and receives 75% of the venture’s net profits, generated by investing USD1 reserves into high-yield U.S. Treasuries currently returning around 3.6%.

Analysts estimate that USD1’s rapid scaling may have added nearly $1 billion to the Trump family’s net worth over the past year, underscoring how stablecoin infrastructure can translate directly into political-adjacent wealth generation.

🚨President Trump’s Net Worth Hits $6.5 Billion, Making Him One of World’s 500 Richest People

Thanks for the "Special Treatment"🤣🤣🤣

Trump's POLLS go up, net worth goes up, and Truth Social goes up 39%.

They will stop at nothing to GET Trump.

Vote for Trump! Prevent WWIII pic.twitter.com/csdPWigWKq

— AJ Huber (@Huberton) March 25, 2024

Binance, for its part, characterizes its role as “standard infrastructure support.” Yet critics warn that such extreme concentration creates systemic tail risk: any regulatory action, technical disruption, or legal challenge facing the exchange could reverberate across nearly the entire USD1 supply.

Ethics Questions Shadow White House and Exchange Ties

The concentration has intensified conflict-of-interest concerns, particularly following reports of a $500 million Emirati investment into WLF just days before the 2025 inauguration, granting a member of Abu Dhabi’s royal family a 49% stake in the venture.

Some lawmakers and watchdog groups have drawn connections between that investment and the subsequent presidential pardon of Binance founder Changpeng Zhao, allegations the White House has repeatedly dismissed. Officials maintain that the President’s business interests are held in a blind trust managed by his children, rejecting claims of “pay-to-play” politics.

Donald Trump doesn't even know who CZ is 👇
So why was CZ pardoned? pic.twitter.com/1vLeabNC72

— Tokio (@Tokio_nb) February 10, 2026

Still, the optics of an exchange holding 87% of a politically linked stablecoin’s supply remain a flashpoint as congressional oversight intensifies.

As World Liberty Financial prepares for its upcoming global forum at Mar-a-Lago on February 18, industry observers are watching closely for signs that the project may seek a U.S. national bank charter — a move that could diversify custody, formalize regulatory standing, and distance the venture from its reliance on the world’s largest offshore exchange.

For now, USD1 stands as a powerful — and controversial — symbol of crypto’s expanding intersection with capital markets, geopolitical capital flows, and executive power.

data.title: The Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) has approved margin financing for virtual assets to enhance market liquidity.
data.description: The Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) issued new guidelines on November 11, allowing licensed brokers (virtual asset brokers) providing virtual asset trading services to expand their related services to include margin financing. A high-level framework has also been established to provide guidance to virtual asset trading platforms, assisting them in developing recommendations for leveraged virtual asset-related products intended for professional investors. The SFC is expanding its product and service diversity in accordance with its ASPIRe roadmap. As one of the latest measures, the SFC now allows virtual asset brokers to provide virtual asset financing services to their securities margin clients, provided they have sufficient collateral and robust investor protection. This will encourage margin clients with sound credit conditions and collateral to participate more actively in virtual asset trading, thereby improving liquidity in the Hong Kong market within a controllable risk framework. For licensed virtual asset trading platforms, the SFC has for the first time developed a high-level framework to guide them in developing perpetual contracts for leveraged products available only to professional investors. This move aims to assist investors in implementing risk management strategies and improve the spot market liquidity of their underlying assets. To ensure investor protection, the framework outlines the high transparency, clear disclosure, and robust operational monitoring measures required for these leveraged products. To further promote virtual asset trading activities in Hong Kong, the Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) permits affiliates of licensed virtual asset trading platforms to act as market makers on their platforms, provided that strong safeguards are in place to reduce conflicts of interest. The participation of these affiliates should provide licensed virtual asset trading platforms with new liquidity channels. Dr. Yip Chi-hang, Executive Director of the Intermediaries Division of the Securities and Futures Commission (SFC), said: “The SFC’s step-by-step development approach, based on the ASPIRe roadmap, is crucial for the large-scale development of Hong Kong’s digital asset market. These targeted measures aim to enhance market liquidity and demonstrate the SFC’s strong commitment to developing Hong Kong’s digital asset market in a sustainable and collaborative manner.” The SFC will continue to closely monitor the implementation of these measures and maintain communication with stakeholders to ensure that they contribute to a safe and competitive market environment in Hong Kong.
